glossary.def: A model of strategic interaction given by a set of players, a strategy set for each player, and a payoff function for each player defined over the full profile of everyone's choices. Each player's reward depends on what all players do, not just on their own move.
The set of strategies that maximize a player's payoff given everyone else's choices held fixed. Almost every equilibrium concept is a fixed point of mutual best response.

A strategy that yields a strictly higher payoff than an alternative against every possible choice of the opponents. A rational player never plays a strictly dominated strategy, so reasoning about the opponent becomes unnecessary.

A strategy profile in which no single player can increase their payoff by unilaterally changing their own strategy. Every finite game has at least one, provided mixed (randomized) strategies are allowed; it predicts stability, not collective optimality.

A two-player game of pure conflict in which one player's gain is exactly the other's loss, so the payoffs sum to zero in every cell. Describable by a single matrix where one player maximizes and the other minimizes.

Von Neumann's result that in any finite two-player zero-sum game, once mixed strategies are allowed, the maximin equals the minimax. Their common value is the value of the game, computable by linear programming.

A probability distribution over a player's pure actions. Payoffs become expected payoffs; randomizing can be strictly safer than any deterministic choice because it leaves the opponent nothing to exploit.

In a mixed equilibrium, every action a player randomizes over must yield the same expected payoff, otherwise they would shift all weight to the better one. A player's mixing probabilities are therefore pinned down by making the opponent indifferent.

refs.cite: von Neumann, J. & Morgenstern, O. (1944). Theory of Games and Economic Behavior.
Nash, J. F. (1950). Equilibrium Points in n-Person Games. PNAS 36(1), 48-49.
Nash, J. F. (1951). Non-Cooperative Games. Annals of Mathematics 54(2), 286-295.
von Neumann, J. (1928). Zur Theorie der Gesellschaftsspiele. Mathematische Annalen 100.
Osborne, M. J. & Rubinstein, A. (1994). A Course in Game Theory. MIT Press.
Easley, D. & Kleinberg, J. (2010). Networks, Crowds, and Markets. Cambridge University Press.
